instruction: Extract all core computer science concepts from the following lecture JSON. Return only a newline-separated list of unique concepts. Do not explain anything.

text_blocks.text: Discrete Structures for Computer
Science
William Garrison
bill@cs.pitt.edu
6311 Sennott Square
Lecture #17: Inclusion/Exclusion, Pigeonhole Principle
Based on materials developed by Dr. Adam Lee

text_blocks.text: Counting Bit Strings
Example: How many bit strings of length 8 start with

runs.text: How many bit strings of length 8 start with

text_blocks.text: a 1 or end with 00?
Solution:
l 27= 128 8-bit strings start with a 1
l 26= 64 8-bit strings end with 00
l 25= 32 8-bit strings start with a 1 and end with 00

runs.text: = 32 8-bit strings start with a 1 and end with 00

text_blocks.text: So, we have 128 + 64 – 32 = 160 ways to construct an
8-bit string that starts with a 1 or ends with 00.

runs.text: 8-bit string that starts with a 1 or ends with 00.

text_blocks.text: Job Applications
Example: A company receives 350 applications.
Suppose 220 of these people majored in CS, 147 majored

runs.text: Suppose 220 of these people majored in CS, 147 majored

text_blocks.text: in business, and 51 were CS/business double-majors.

runs.text: in business, and 51 were CS/business double-majors.

text_blocks.text: How many applicants majored in neither CS nor business?

runs.text: How many applicants majored in neither CS nor business?

text_blocks.text: Solution:
l Let C be the set of CS majors, B be the set of business majors

runs.text: Let C be the set of CS majors, B be the set of business majors

text_blocks.text: l
l
So of the 350 applications, 350 – 316 = 34 applications
neither majored in CS nor business.

input.title: The pigeonhole principle can be used to prove a

text_blocks.text: The pigeonhole principle can be used to prove a

text_blocks.text: number of interesting results
Claim:  Every integer n has a multiple whose decimal

runs.text: Every integer n has a multiple whose decimal

text_blocks.text: representation contains only 1s and 0s
Proof:
l Let n be a positive integer

runs.text: Let n be a positive integer

text_blocks.text: l Consider the set of n+1 integers S = {1, 11, 111, …, 111…1}

runs.text: Consider the set of n+1 integers S = {1, 11, 111, …, 111…1}

text_blocks.text: l Note that when any integer x is divided by n, there are n

runs.text: Note that when any integer x is divided by n, there are n

text_blocks.text: possible remainders (0 through n-1)
l Since S contains n+1 elements, at least two elements of S

runs.text: Since S contains n+1 elements, at least two elements of S

text_blocks.text: have the same remainder when divided by n (call them x

runs.text: have the same remainder when divided by n (call them x

text_blocks.text: and y, with x > y)
l Since x ≡y (mod n), n | (x – y), and thus na = (x – y)

runs.text: y (mod n), n | (x – y), and thus na = (x – y)

text_blocks.text: l Finally, we note that x – y contains only 0s and 1s  ❏

runs.text: Finally, we note that x – y contains only 0s and 1s

input.title: There is a more general form of the pigeonhole

text_blocks.text: There is a more general form of the pigeonhole

runs.text: There is a more general form of the pigeonhole

text_blocks.text: principle that is even more useful

runs.text: principle that is even more useful

text_blocks.text: The generalized pigeonhole principle:  If N objects are
placed into k boxes, then there is at least one box

runs.text: placed into k boxes, then there is at least one box

text_blocks.text: containing at least ⌈N/k⌉items.
Proof:
l Assume that no box contains more than ⌈N/k⌉- 1 objects

runs.text: Assume that no box contains more than

text_blocks.text: l Note that ⌈N/k⌉< (N/k) + 1
l This contradicts our assumption that we had N objects  ❏

runs.text: This contradicts our assumption that we had N objects

text_blocks.text: Example
What is the minimum number of students needed to

runs.text: What is the minimum number of students needed to

text_blocks.text: guarantee that at least six students receive the same

runs.text: guarantee that at least six students receive the same

text_blocks.text: grade, if possible grades are A, B, C, D, and F?

runs.text: grade, if possible grades are A, B, C, D, and F?

text_blocks.text: Solution:
l
Need the smallest integer N such that ⌈N/5⌉= 6

runs.text: Need the smallest integer N such that

text_blocks.text: l
With 25 students, it would be possible (though maybe

runs.text: With 25 students, it would be possible (though maybe

text_blocks.text: unlikely) to have 5 students get each possible grade

runs.text: unlikely) to have 5 students get each possible grade

text_blocks.text: l
By adding a 26th student, we guarantee that at least 6

runs.text: By adding a 26th student, we guarantee that at least 6

text_blocks.text: students get one possible grade
l
So, the smallest such N is  5 × 5 + 1 = 26  ❏

runs.text: So, the smallest such N is  5

l Let’s make 4 piles: one for each suit

l We can do this using 4 × 2 + 1 = 9 cards
Note:  We don’t need 9 cards to end up with three

text_blocks.text: from the same suit—if we did, we could never get a

runs.text: from the same suit—if we did, we could never get a

“objects”
